content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

March 3, 1977

Mr. Richard T. Gleed
4547 Laurelwood Way
Sacramento, California 95825

Dear Mr. Gleed:

This is in response to your letter of February 22, 1977,
in which you request the Fair Political Practices Commission
to direct the Franchise Tax Board (FTB) to release for your
inspection petitions of Board employees requesting exemption
from or modification of the Conflict of Interest Code of the
Franchise Tax Board approved by the Fair Political Practices
Commission on September 21, 1976.

On February 10, 1977, you requested copies of petitions
for amendment submitted to the FTB by employees pursuant to
Government Code §§87307 and 81008. On February 17, 1977, the
Franchise Tax Board denied your request, because in its view,
Government Code §81008 did not require the production for
public inspection of a "voluntary statement filed by an
employee seeking an administrative review of designated
status".

Government Code §81008 provides that "Every report and
statement filed pursuant to this title is a public record
open for public inspection and reproduction during regular
business hours, commencing as soon as practicable, but in
any event not later than the second business day following
the day on which it was received. No conditions whatsoever
shall be imposed upon persons desiring to inspect or reproduce
reports and statements filed under this title, nor shall any
information or identification be required from such persons".
The clear import of §81008, and indeed, all references to
"reports" or "statements" in Government Code §§31001-81010
lead to the conclusion that petitions, whether formal or
informal, filed with an agency pursuant to Government Code
§87307, are not "statements" or "reports" within Government
Code §81008.

810008

Richard T. Gleed
March 3, 1977
Page Two


     In the event that review of petitions submitted by
designated employees within the Code of the Board demonstrates the necessity for amendment to the Code as adopted
and approved, the Board, prior to submitting its proposed
amendments to the Fair Political Practices Commission, will
be obliged to hold a public hearing regarding proposed
changes in the Code. In this event, any interested person
may secure, pursuant to the Administrative Procedure Act,
relevant documents or materials necessary for the full
participation of such person in the public hearing regarding the Board's proposed amendment. If, however,
the Board determines that petitions before it are without
merit, there is no need to hold a public hearing since no
amendment would be contemplated. Based upon the response
of Mr. Martin Huff of February 16, 1977, to your request,
it is apparent that the Board views petitions for modification or removal from a Code as an internal personnel
matter until such time as the Board decides to act upon
the petitions or until the petition has been denied and
appeal taken to the code reviewing body. This seems both
a reasonable and permissible point of view, the alternative
to which would be a never-ending series of petitions for
review of designations when such review should have taken
place in the context of Code development.

     I am informed also that any petitioning employee may
request that you or the California State Employee's Association be permitted an opportunity to view his or her
petition during the pendancy of the Board's review of
his or her petition.

     Pursuant to Government Code §87307, a petitioning
employee may appeal to the code reviewing body for a
review of the action or non-action of the agency whose
Code is in question. It is the intention of this division
to recommend to the Commission that persons seeking appeal
before the Commission pursuant to Government Code §87307
be given an opportunity for an appropriate hearing. Thus,
all petitions submitted to the Fair Political Practices
Commission calendared for hearing will be open for public
inspection for a reasonable period prior to any hearing
thereon.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:glb
cc:  Jack Gordon
     Noel Robinson

Richard T. Gleed
4547 Laurelwood Way
Sacramento, California 95825


February 22, 1977


Delbert Spurlock, Chief
Conflict of Interest Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

During January 1977 employees of the Franchise Tax Board submitted
petitions to the Board requesting an amendment to the Department's
Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code section 87307
(a copy of the form used by the employees is enclosed). According
to the Department approximately forty such petitions were submitted.

On February 10, 1977 I requested copies of the petitions submitted
by employees pursuant to Government Code section 81008 (see
enclosed note). On February 16, 1977 the Franchise Tax Board denied
my request stating that Government Code section 81008 referred only to
financial statements required by law and did not extend to voluntary
statement filed by employees seeking administrative review of
their designated status. (a copy of letter enclosed)

I do not believe that the Franchise Tax Board has acted in accordance
with the law and, therefore, I request that the Board be directed
to immediately comply with the provisions of Government Code section
81008.

If you have any questions regarding this request, please contact me
at 444-8134.

Sincerely,

R. T. Gleed

R. T. Gleed

western union                                    Telofax

CALL
LETTERS      FBG          CHARGE    CSEA         [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]

                                                                    ( 1 of 2 )
                                                          EXPERT DELIVERY
Mr. Noel J. Robinson, Counsel
Franchise Tax Board
Aerojet Center
Aerojet/Nimbus
Sacramento, California

Pursuant to Government Code Section 81008 I am formally
asking for a copy of each petition requesting amendment
of the conflict of interest code for the Franchise Tax Board
filed by employees of the board in accordance with the
provisions of paragraph (g) (4) (A) of Section 17005, Title
18, California Administrative Code


                                    SENDING BLANK
    Send the above message, subject to terms on back hereof, which are hereby agreed to

PLEASE TYPE OR WRITE PLAINLY WITHIN BORDER-DO NOT FOLD
WU 1263 (10/60)


        western union                                    Telofax

CALL
LETTERS      FBG          CHARGE    [illegible]    [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]


                                                                    ( 2 of 2 )

I am prepared to appear at your facility and pay for the requested
copies in accordance with the law.


Richard T. Glead
4547 Laurelwood Way
Sacramento, CA 95825


                                    SENDING BLANK
    Send the above message, subject to terms on back hereof, which are hereby agreed to

PLEASE TYPE OR WRITE PLAINLY WITHIN BORDER-DO NOT FOLD
WU 1263 (10/60)

STATE OF CALIFORNIA

FRANCHISE TAX BOARD
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95827
TELEPHONE: (916) 355-0529


February 16, 1977


Richard T. Gleed
California State Employees Association
1103 O Street
Sacramento, CA  95814


This will acknowledge your formal demand to inspect and copy petitions of
employees who seek an administrative review of their designated status under
the Franchise Tax Board's Conflict of Interest Code.

Your demand relies on Government Code, Section 81008, as authority to inspect
and copy such petitions.  Because of the location and structure of that section
in the Political Reform Act, we view it as applying to the reporting required
of candidates, committees, lobbyists, and public officials (including designated
employees) but not to the voluntary statement filed by an employee seeking an
administrative review of designated status.

We will, however, inform those employees, whose petitions have been denied, of
CSEA's interest in their case and will furnish them with your telephone number
should they wish to contact you.


Martin Huff
Executive Officer

sections.question: During January 1977 employees of the Franchise Tax Board submitted petitions to the Board requesting an amendment to the Department's Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code section 87307. On February 10, 1977 the requestor asked for copies of the petitions submitted by the employees pursuant to Government Code section 81008. On February 16, 1977 the Franchise Tax Board denied the request stating that Government Code section 51005 referred only to financial statements required by law and did not extend to voluntary statements filed by employees seeking administrative review of their designated status. The requestor asked that the Board be directed to immediately comply with the provisions of Government Code section 81008.
sections.conclusion: Petitions, whether formal or informal, filed with an agency pursuant to Government Code section 87307, are not 'statements' or 'reports' within Government Code section 81008. The Franchise Tax Board's view that petitions for modification or removal from a code are an internal personnel matter until such time as the Board decides to act upon the petitions is both reasonable and permissible. However, if the Board determines to act on petitions and proposes amendments, a public hearing must be held, and all petitions submitted to the FPPC calendared for hearing will be open for public inspection for a reasonable period prior to any hearing.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether employee petitions requesting amendments to a Conflict of Interest Code are public records subject to inspection under Government Code section 81008, or whether they are internal personnel matters exempt from disclosure.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that employee petitions for amendments to a Conflict of Interest Code are not public records under Government Code section 81008, and may be treated as internal personnel matters. However, if the Board proposes amendments based on petitions, a public hearing is required and petitions will become available for public inspection at that time.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request (February 22, 1977 letter from Gleed), FPPC response (March 3, 1977 letter from Spurlock), and supporting materials including Franchise Tax Board denial letter. Some text is difficult to read due to image quality, but main legal question and conclusion are clear. The document ID '77ADV-77-125' and advice letter classification are accurate.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: During January 1977 employees of the Franchise Tax Board submitted petitions to the Board requesting an amendment to the Department's Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code section 87307. On February 10, 1977 the requestor asked for copies of the petitions submitted by the employees pursuant to Government Code section 81008. On February 16, 1977 the Franchise Tax Board denied the request stating that Government Code section 51005 referred only to financial statements required by law and did not extend to voluntary statements filed by employees seeking administrative review of their designated status. The requestor asked that the Board be directed to immediately comply with the provisions of Government Code section 81008.

CONCLUSION: Petitions, whether formal or informal, filed with an agency pursuant to Government Code section 87307, are not 'statements' or 'reports' within Government Code section 81008. The Franchise Tax Board's view that petitions for modification or removal from a code are an internal personnel matter until such time as the Board decides to act upon the petitions is both reasonable and permissible. However, if the Board determines to act on petitions and proposes amendments, a public hearing must be held, and all petitions submitted to the FPPC calendared for hearing will be open for public inspection for a reasonable period prior to any hearing.

On February 10, 1977, you requested copies of petitions for amendment submitted to the FTB by employees pursuant to Government Code §§87307 and 81008. On February 17, 1977, the Franchise Tax Board denied your request, because in its view, Government Code §81008 did not require the production for public inspection of a "voluntary statement filed by an employee seeking an administrative review of designated status". Government Code §81008 provides that "Every report and statement filed pursuant to this title is a public record open for public inspection and reproduction during regular business hours, commencing as soon as practicable, but in any event not later than the second business day following the day on which it was received. No conditions whatsoever shall be imposed upon persons desiring to inspect or reproduce reports and statements filed under this title, nor shall any information or identification be required from such persons". The clear import of §81008, and indeed, all references to "reports" or "statements" in Government Code §§31001-81010 lead to the conclusion that petitions, whether formal or informal, filed with an agency pursuant to Government Code §87307, are not "statements" or "reports" within Government Code §81008. 810008 Richard T. Gleed March 3, 1977 Page Two In the event that review of petitions submitted by designated employees within the Code of the Board demonstrates the necessity for amendment to the Code as adopted and approved, the Board, prior to submitting its proposed amendments to the Fair Political Practices Commission, will be obliged to hold a public hearing regarding proposed changes in the Code. If, however, the Board determines that petitions before it are without merit, there is no need to hold a public hearing since no amendment would be contemplated. Based upon the response of Mr. Martin Huff of February 16, 1977, to your request, it is apparent that the Board views petitions for modification or removal from a Code as an internal personnel matter until such time as the Board decides to act upon the petitions or until the petition has been denied and appeal taken to the code reviewing body.
embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that employee petitions requesting amendments to the Franchise Tax Board's Conflict of Interest Code are internal personnel matters not subject to mandatory public disclosure under Government Code section 81008. The FPPC affirmed the Board's position while clarifying that if proposed amendments result, public hearings must be held and petitions become subject to inspection.
